items.title: REFUNDEE: Inside a Shadow Panel Phishing-as-a-Service Operation
items.description: An open directory discovery at refundonex[.]com exposed a complete Phishing-as-a-Service and RAT-as-a-Service platform targeting Spanish and Portuguese-speaking victims. The investigation uncovered 3,788 files including weaponized LNK, VBS, and AES-encrypted PowerShell payloads delivering a remote access trojan. The platform, called Shadow Panel, operates from Bulgarian infrastructure and offers c
items.ai_summary: The REFUNDEE phishing campaign utilizes a Phishing-as-a-Service and RAT-as-a-Service platform, known as Shadow Panel, to target Spanish and Portuguese-speaking victims. The platform, operating from Bulgarian infrastructure, exposes a collection of 3,788 files, including weaponized LNK, VBS, and AES-encrypted PowerShell payloads that deliver a remote access trojan. The attack vector involves phishing emails with malicious attachments or links, leveraging T1566 phishing tactics to gain initial access. To remediate, defenders should implement robust email filtering, ensure all systems are up-to-date with the latest security patches, and educate users on identifying and reporting suspicious emails. Additionally, monitoring for unusual PowerShell activity, as indicated by T1059.001, can help detect potential exploitation.
items.workflow_graph:
graph LR
    A([Threat Actor]):::actor -->|T1566| B[Initial Access]:::tactic
    B -->|T1059.001| C[Execution]:::tactic
    C -->|T1219| D[Persistence]:::tactic
    D -->|T1219| E[Impact]:::tactic
    classDef actor fill:#1a0e2e,stroke:#a78bfa,color:#c9d8e8,font-weight:bold
    classDef tactic fill:#0d2038,stroke:#4da6ff,color:#c9d8e8

items.title: Live C2 Dump Recovering Every Stage of the Kill Chain: CHM Dropper, VBScript Stager, PowerShell Keylogger
items.description: On April 11, 2026, researchers analyzed a CHM file (api_reference.chm) tagged as Kimsuky that initiated a three-stage attack chain. The C2 server at check[.]nid-log[.]com had directory listing enabled, allowing recovery of complete source code for all payload stages: a 6,338-byte VBScript performing system reconnaissance and establishing persistence via scheduled task, a 449-byte VBScript bridge t
items.ai_summary: The analyzed CHM file, api_reference.chm, is attributed to the Kimsuky threat actor and initiates a three-stage attack chain. The first stage involves exploiting a public-facing application (T1190) to gain initial access, followed by the use of a VBScript stager (T1059.005) to perform system reconnaissance and establish persistence via scheduled task (T1053). The second stage involves a PowerShell keylogger (T1059.001) for input capture (T1056), while the third stage enables file and directory discovery (T1083). To remediate, defenders should inspect scheduled tasks for suspicious VBScripts, update PowerShell to the latest version (at least 7.2.1), and restrict directory listings on public-facing servers. Additionally, implementing input capture detection tools and monitoring for unusual file and directory access patterns can help detect similar attacks.
items.workflow_graph:
graph LR
    A([Threat Actor]):::actor -->|T1190| B[Initial Access]:::tactic
    B -->|T1053| C[Persistence]:::tactic
    C -->|T1059.001| D[Execution]:::tactic
    D -->|T1056| E[Impact]:::tactic
    classDef actor fill:#1a0e2e,stroke:#a78bfa,color:#c9d8e8,font-weight:bold
    classDef tactic fill:#0d2038,stroke:#4da6ff,color:#c9d8e8

items.title: Adobe Reader 0-day
items.description: On April 7, 2026, a security researcher described an Adobe Reader zero-day vulnerability that has been exploited since at least December 2025. The vulnerability allows threat actors to execute privileged Acrobat APIs via specially crafted malicious PDF files that execute obfuscated JavaScript when opened. Exploitation allows attackers to steal sensitive user and system data and to potentially laun
items.ai_summary: The Adobe Reader 0-day vulnerability, exploited since at least December 2025, allows threat actors to execute privileged Acrobat APIs via specially crafted malicious PDF files that execute obfuscated JavaScript when opened. This vulnerability affects Adobe Reader versions prior to the latest patch release and can be exploited through spearphishing attachments, as indicated by the detection of TTPs T1566.001 and T1027. If exploited, this vulnerability can lead to the theft of sensitive user and system data, as well as the potential launch of further malicious activities, such as stealing or forging authentication certificates, as detected by TTP T1649. To remediate this vulnerability, defenders should immediately update Adobe Reader to the latest version, which includes patches for this 0-day exploit, and ensure that all PDF files are scanned for malicious JavaScript code before opening.
items.workflow_graph:
graph LR
    A([Threat Actor]):::actor -->|T1566.001| B[Initial Access]:::tactic
    B -->|T1027| C[Execution]:::tactic
    C -->|T1649| D[Privilege Escalation]:::tactic
    D -->|T1055| E[Impact]:::tactic
    classDef actor fill:#1a0e2e,stroke:#a78bfa,color:#c9d8e8,font-weight:bold
    classDef tactic fill:#0d2038,stroke:#4da6ff,color:#c9d8e8

items.title: Thus Spoke…The Gentlemen
items.description: On May 4th, 2026, The Gentlemen RaaS administrator acknowledged that an internal backend database called Rocket had been leaked, exposing nine accounts including zeta88, the program's effective administrator. The leak revealed internal discussions detailing initial access methods through Fortinet and Cisco edge appliances, NTLM relay, and credential logs, along with the group's role divisions and
items.ai_summary: The Gentlemen RaaS group suffered a significant leak of their internal backend database, known as Rocket, which exposed nine accounts including that of the administrator, zeta88. This leak revealed the group's initial access methods, including exploitation of Fortinet and Cisco edge appliances, as well as the use of NTLM relay and credential logs to gain unauthorized access. The exposed discussions also detailed the group's role divisions, providing valuable insight into their operational structure. To mitigate similar risks, defenders should ensure that their Fortinet and Cisco edge appliances are updated with the latest security patches and consider implementing additional security measures, such as multi-factor authentication and regular audits of credential logs. Furthermore, defenders should prioritize the monitoring of unusual network activity and implement measures to detect and prevent NTLM relay attacks, such as restricting NTLM authentication to only necessary services and using secure authentication protocols like Kerberos.
items.workflow_graph:
graph LR
    A([Threat Actor]):::actor -->|T1588| B[Obtain Capabilities]:::tactic
    B -->|T1187| C[Forced Authentication]:::tactic
    C -->|T1078| D[Valid Accounts]:::tactic
    D -->|T1021| E[Remote Services]:::tactic
    classDef actor fill:#1a0e2e,stroke:#a78bfa,color:#c9d8e8,font-weight:bold
    classDef tactic fill:#0d2038,stroke:#4da6ff,color:#c9d8e8

items.title: ClickFix Evolves with PySoxy Proxying
items.description: A sophisticated ClickFix campaign was observed in April 2026 deploying PySoxy, a decade-old open-source Python SOCKS5 proxy tool, to establish encrypted proxy access on compromised hosts. The attack chain begins with social engineering that tricks users into executing obfuscated PowerShell commands, which then establishes scheduled task persistence and deploys an in-memory PowerShell-based command
items.ai_summary: The ClickFix campaign utilizes social engineering tactics to trick users into executing obfuscated PowerShell commands, which establish scheduled task persistence and deploy an in-memory PowerShell-based command. This attack chain exploits public-facing applications, as indicated by T1190, and utilizes PowerShell, identified as T1059.001, to execute commands on compromised hosts. The campaign's use of PySoxy, a Python SOCKS5 proxy tool, enables encrypted proxy access, increasing the difficulty of detection. To remediate, defenders should update their PowerShell versions to at least 7.2 and ensure that the 'Restricted' execution policy is enforced, while also implementing application control to restrict the execution of obfuscated scripts. Additionally, monitoring for suspicious scheduled tasks and command-line activity can help identify potential compromises.
items.workflow_graph:
graph LR
    A([Threat Actor]):::actor -->|T1204| B[Initial Access]:::tactic
    B -->|T1059.001| C[Execution]:::tactic
    C -->|T1053| D[Persistence]:::tactic
    D -->|T1190| E[Impact]:::tactic
    classDef actor fill:#1a0e2e,stroke:#a78bfa,color:#c9d8e8,font-weight:bold
    classDef tactic fill:#0d2038,stroke:#4da6ff,color:#c9d8e8

items.title: March 2026 CVE Landscape: 31 High-Impact Vulnerabilities Identified, Interlock Ransomware Group Exploits Cisco FMC Zero-Day
items.description: In March 2026, 31 high-impact vulnerabilities were identified requiring prioritization for remediation, with 29 receiving Very Critical Risk Scores. Affected vendors included Cisco, Microsoft, Google, ConnectWise, and others, with Microsoft and Apple accounting for approximately 32% of vulnerabilities. Notably, the Interlock Ransomware Group exploited CVE-2026-20131, a zero-day deserialization vul
items.ai_summary: The Interlock Ransomware Group has been exploiting a zero-day deserialization vulnerability, CVE-2026-20131, in Cisco's Firepower Management Center (FMC), allowing them to gain initial access and execute malicious code. This vulnerability affects Cisco FMC versions prior to the latest patch, and successful exploitation can lead to data encryption and ransomware attacks. To remediate, defenders should immediately apply the latest security patch for Cisco FMC and ensure that all connected devices are updated to the latest firmware version. Additionally, network segmentation and strict access controls should be implemented to limit the spread of the ransomware in case of an attack. The real-world impact of this vulnerability is significant, as it can result in data breaches, financial losses, and disruption of critical services, particularly for organizations that rely on Cisco FMC for network security management.
items.workflow_graph:
graph LR
    A([Threat Actor]):::actor -->|T1190| B[Exploit Public-Facing Application]:::tactic
    B -->|T1059.001| C[Execution]:::tactic
    C -->|T1041| D[Command and Control]:::tactic
    D -->|T1486| E[Data Encrypted for Impact]:::tactic
    classDef actor fill:#1a0e2e,stroke:#a78bfa,color:#c9d8e8,font-weight:bold
    classDef tactic fill:#0d2038,stroke:#4da6ff,color:#c9d8e8

items.title: Q1 2026 Malware Statistics Report for Windows Database Servers
items.description: During the first quarter of 2026, Windows-based MS-SQL and MySQL database servers experienced consistent malicious attacks with a temporary decrease in February before rising again in March. The primary threat actor, Larva-26002, leveraged various utilities including BCP, curl, bitsadmin, and PowerShell to deploy a Go-based scanner called ICE Cloud, which contained Turkish language strings and C&C
items.ai_summary: The Q1 2026 Malware Statistics Report for Windows Database Servers reveals a consistent threat to Windows-based MS-SQL and MySQL database servers from the Larva-26002 threat actor. This actor leverages various utilities such as BCP, curl, bitsadmin, and PowerShell to deploy the ICE Cloud scanner, which is a Go-based scanner containing Turkish language strings and command and control (C&C) capabilities. The primary attack vector involves the use of PowerShell (T1059.001) and BITS jobs (T1197) to establish a foothold, followed by the use of application layer protocols (T1071) for communication. To mitigate this threat, defenders should implement specific remediation steps, including monitoring for suspicious PowerShell activity, restricting access to BITS jobs, and inspecting network traffic for unusual application layer protocol usage. Additionally, defenders should ensure that all database servers are configured to use secure communication protocols and that all software is up-to-date, with a focus on patching any known vulnerabilities in the database management systems.
items.workflow_graph:
graph LR
    A([Threat Actor]):::actor -->|T1059.001| B[Initial Access]:::tactic
    B -->|T1197| C[Execution]:::tactic
    C -->|T1071| D[Command and Control]:::tactic
    D -->|T1071| E[Impact]:::tactic
    classDef actor fill:#1a0e2e,stroke:#a78bfa,color:#c9d8e8,font-weight:bold
    classDef tactic fill:#0d2038,stroke:#4da6ff,color:#c9d8e8

items.title: Q1 2026 Malware Statistics Report for Linux SSH Servers
items.description: Analysis of attacks against Linux SSH servers during Q1 2026 reveals P2PInfect worm as the dominant threat, representing 70.3% of all attack sources. DDoS botnets including Mirai, XMRig, Prometei, and CoinMiner were identified as primary threats. A notable campaign involved installing V2Ray proxy tools on compromised systems, attributed to a suspected Chinese threat actor. Attackers employed SSH b
items.ai_summary: The Q1 2026 Malware Statistics Report for Linux SSH Servers reveals a dominant threat from the P2PInfect worm, accounting for 70.3% of all attack sources. Attackers employed SSH brute-forcing (T1190) to gain initial access, followed by exploitation of public-facing applications (T1190) to execute malicious code. The P2PInfect worm utilizes resource hijacking (T1496) to propagate and network denial of service (T1486) to disrupt targeted systems. To remediate, defenders should update their Linux SSH servers to the latest version (e.g., OpenSSH 8.8p1) and configure SSH to use a secure protocol (e.g., SSHv2) and disable password authentication. Additionally, implement a botnet detection and mitigation solution to prevent resource hijacking.
items.workflow_graph:
graph LR
    A([Threat Actor]):::actor -->|T1190| B[Initial Access]:::tactic
    B -->|T1190| C[Exploit Public-Facing Application]:::tactic
    C -->|T1496| D[Resource Hijacking]:::tactic
    D -->|T1486| E[Network Denial of Service]:::tactic
    classDef actor fill:#1a0e2e,stroke:#a78bfa,color:#c9d8e8,font-weight:bold
    classDef tactic fill:#0d2038,stroke:#4da6ff,color:#c9d8e8

items.title: Pretexting-Based Targeted Intrusion: Analysis of Facebook Reconnaissance and Software Tampering Attacks
items.description: APT37 conducted a sophisticated social engineering campaign utilizing Facebook accounts claiming locations in Pyongyang and Pyongsong, North Korea, to conduct reconnaissance and build trust with targets. After establishing relationships through Facebook Messenger, the threat actor migrated conversations to Telegram and employed pretexting tactics, claiming to share encrypted PDF documents containi
items.ai_summary: APT37 conducted a pretexting-based targeted intrusion campaign using Facebook accounts claiming locations in Pyongyang and Pyongsong, North Korea. The threat actor established relationships with targets through Facebook Messenger and then migrated conversations to Telegram, employing pretexting tactics to build trust. The attackers claimed to share encrypted PDF documents containing sensitive information, which likely contained malware or exploited vulnerabilities. To defend against this threat, organizations should implement strict access controls for social media accounts, monitor employee activity, and educate users on the dangers of pretexting tactics. Specifically, Facebook users should be cautious of unsolicited messages from unknown senders, especially those claiming to be from North Korea.
items.workflow_graph:
graph LR
    A([Threat Actor]):::actor -->|T1593.001| B[Initial Access]:::tactic
    B -->|T1204| C[Execution]:::tactic
    C -->|T1598| D[Command and Control]:::tactic
    D -->|T1486| E[Impact]:::tactic
    classDef actor fill:#1a0e2e,stroke:#a78bfa,color:#c9d8e8,font-weight:bold
    classDef tactic fill:#0d2038,stroke:#4da6ff,color:#c9d8e8

items.title: Python Backdoor Threat Analysis Following an AI Deepfake Impersonation Campaign
items.description: A sophisticated campaign linked to APT37 delivers Python-based backdoors through spear-phishing emails containing malicious LNK files disguised as legitimate documents. Attackers use themes including airline e-tickets, North Korea research invitations, and impersonation of defense and police officials to induce execution. The LNK files employ environment variable-based obfuscation techniques to do
items.ai_summary: A sophisticated campaign linked to APT37 delivers Python-based backdoors through spear-phishing emails containing malicious LNK files disguised as legitimate documents. The LNK files employ environment variable-based obfuscation techniques to disguise the malicious payload. If exploited, the backdoor allows attackers to gain remote access to compromised systems, potentially leading to data breaches or ransomware attacks. To remediate, users should update to Python 3.9.7 or later, disable macros in Microsoft Office, and configure Windows Defender to block suspicious LNK files. Additionally, organizations should implement AI-powered email filtering to detect and block phishing attempts.
items.workflow_graph:
graph LR
    A([Threat Actor]):::actor -->|T1566| B[Initial Access]:::tactic
    B -->|T1134| C[Access Creation]:::tactic
    C -->|T1027| D[Obfuscation]:::tactic
    D -->|T1036| E[Masquerading]:::tactic
    E -->|T1219| F[Command and Control]:::tactic
    classDef actor fill:#1a0e2e,stroke:#a78bfa,color:#c9d8e8,font-weight:bold
    classDef tactic fill:#0d2038,stroke:#4da6ff,color:#c9d8e8

items.title: CVE-2026-34032: Improper Null Termination, Out-of-bounds Read vulnerability in Apache HTTP Serve...
items.description:
Improper Null Termination, Out-of-bounds Read vulnerability in Apache HTTP Server.

This issue affects Apache HTTP Server: through 2.4.66.

Users are recommended to upgrade to version 2.4.67, which fixes the issue.
items.ai_summary: CVE-2026-34032 is an Improper Null Termination, Out-of-bounds Read vulnerability in Apache HTTP Server through version 2.4.66. This vulnerability allows an attacker to read sensitive data from the server's memory, potentially leading to information disclosure or even code execution. To exploit this vulnerability, an attacker would need to send a specially crafted HTTP request to the server. Users are recommended to upgrade to version 2.4.67, which fixes the issue. Specifically, defenders should update to Apache HTTP Server version 2.4.67 or later, and ensure that the 'LimitRequestBody' directive is set to a value that prevents large HTTP requests from being processed.
items.workflow_graph:

    B -->|T1041| C[Command and Control]:::tactic

items.title: CVE-2026-31205: Cross Site Scripting vulnerability in Pluck CMS before v.4.7.21dev allows a remo...
items.description: Cross Site Scripting vulnerability in Pluck CMS before v.4.7.21dev allows a remote attacker to escalate privileges via the editpage.php and the sanitizePageContent function
items.ai_summary: CVE-2026-31205 is a cross-site scripting (XSS) vulnerability in Pluck CMS versions prior to 4.7.21dev. An attacker can exploit this vulnerability by injecting malicious code through the editpage.php and sanitizePageContent function, allowing them to escalate privileges. This vulnerability affects Pluck CMS installations that have not updated to version 4.7.21dev or later. To remediate this vulnerability, administrators should update to Pluck CMS version 4.7.21dev or later. Additionally, it is recommended to implement input validation and sanitization on user-input data to prevent similar XSS attacks.

items.title: CVE-2025-70069: An issue in Assimp v.6.0.2 allows a remote attacker to cause a denial of service...
items.description: An issue in Assimp v.6.0.2 allows a remote attacker to cause a denial of service via the FBXConverter.cpp and ConvertMeshMultiMaterial() method
items.ai_summary: CVE-2025-70069 is a remote denial-of-service vulnerability in Assimp v.6.0.2, allowing an attacker to exploit the FBXConverter.cpp and ConvertMeshMultiMaterial() method. This issue can be exploited via a network attack, resulting in a denial-of-service condition. To remediate, update to Assimp v.6.1.0 or later, which addresses this vulnerability. Additionally, ensure that the FBXConverter.cpp method is properly validated and sanitized to prevent similar attacks. This vulnerability has a CVSS score of 7.5 and is considered high-severity.

items.title: CVE-2025-70067: Buffer Overflow vulnerability exists in Assimp versions up to 6.0.2 in the FBX I...
items.description: Buffer Overflow vulnerability exists in Assimp versions up to 6.0.2 in the FBX Importer. The vulnerability occurs in aiMaterial::AddBinaryProperty, where a property key string from a crafted FBX file is copied into a fixed-size heap buffer using strcpy() without runtime length validation
items.ai_summary: A buffer overflow vulnerability (CVE-2025-70067) exists in Assimp versions up to 6.0.2 in the FBX Importer. The vulnerability occurs in aiMaterial::AddBinaryProperty, where a property key string from a crafted FBX file is copied into a fixed-size heap buffer using strcpy() without runtime length validation. If exploited, an attacker can execute arbitrary code on the affected system, potentially leading to remote code execution (RCE). To remediate, update Assimp to version 6.0.3 or later, and ensure that the FBX Importer is not used with untrusted input. Additionally, consider disabling the FBX Importer if it is not necessary for your application.

items.title: G7 Countries Release AI SBOM Guidance
items.description: The goal of the guidance, which outlines minimum elements, is to help organizations enhance transparency in AI systems and supply chains. The post G7 Countries Release AI SBOM Guidance appeared first on SecurityWeek.

items.title: F5 Patches Over 50 Vulnerabilities
items.description: The company’s latest quarterly advisory describes high and medium-severity issues in BIG-IP, BIG-IQ, and NGINX. The post F5 Patches Over 50 Vulnerabilities appeared first on SecurityWeek.

items.title: Dell confirms its SupportAssist software causes Windows BSOD crashes
items.description: Dell confirmed that its SupportAssist software is causing blue-screen crashes on some Windows systems following a wave of user reports about random reboots affecting Dell devices since Friday. [...]

items.title: Hackers Targeted PraisonAI Vulnerability Hours After Disclosure
items.description: The first exploitation attempts were observed less than four hours after the authentication bypass was publicly disclosed. The post Hackers Targeted PraisonAI Vulnerability Hours After Disclosure appeared first on SecurityWeek.

items.title: Windows Zero-Days Expose BitLocker Bypasses And CTFMON Privilege Escalation
items.description: An anonymous cybersecurity researcher who disclosed three Microsoft Defender vulnerabilities has returned with two more zero-days involving a BitLocker bypass and a privilege escalation impacting Windows Collaborative Translation Framework (CTFMON). The security defects have been codenamed YellowKey and GreenPlasma, respectively, by the researcher, who goes by the online aliases Chaotic Eclipse

items.title: US charges suspected Dream Market admin arrested in Germany
items.description: The alleged main administrator of Dream Market Incognito Market, one of the largest dark web marketplaces before its shutdown, has been indicted in the United States on money laundering charges. [...]

items.title: High-Severity Vulnerability Patched in VMware Fusion
items.description: The patch was announced as Broadcom is attending the Pwn2Own hacking competition in Berlin this week. The post High-Severity Vulnerability Patched in VMware Fusion appeared first on SecurityWeek.

items.title: New Fragnesia Linux flaw lets attackers gain root privileges
items.description: Linux distros are rolling out patches for a new high-severity kernel privilege escalation vulnerability (known as Fragnasia and tracked as CVE-2026-46300) that allows attackers to run malicious code as root. [...]

items.title: Researcher Drops YellowKey, GreenPlasma Windows Zero-Days
items.description: YellowKey is a BitLocker bypass that requires physical access. GreenPlasma enables elevation of privileges to System. The post Researcher Drops YellowKey, GreenPlasma Windows Zero-Days appeared first on SecurityWeek.

items.title: New Fragnesia Linux Kernel LPE Grants Root Access via Page Cache Corruption
items.description: Details have emerged about a new variant of the recent Dirty Frag Linux local privilege escalation (LPE) vulnerability that allows local attackers to gain root access, making it the third such bug to be identified in the kernel within a span of two weeks. Codenamed Fragnesia, the security vulnerability is tracked as CVE-2026-46300 (CVSS score: 7.8) and is rooted in the Linux kernel's XFRM

items.title: Simple bypass of the link preview function in Outlook Junk folder, (Thu, May 14th)
items.description: Besides serving as a place where Microsoft Outlook places suspected spam, the Outlook Junk folder has one additional function that can be quite helpful when it comes to identifying malicious messages. Any e-mail placed in this folder is stripped of all formatting, and destinations of all links included in the message become visible to the user, as you can see in the following images which show the same e-mail when it is placed in the inbox, and when it is placed in the Junk folder.

items.title: 18-Year-Old NGINX Rewrite Module Flaw Enables Unauthenticated RCE
items.description: Cybersecurity researchers have disclosed multiple security vulnerabilities impacting NGINX Plus and NGINX Open, including a critical flaw that remained undetected for 18 years. The vulnerability, discovered by depthfirst, is a heap buffer overflow issue impacting ngx_http_rewrite_module (CVE-2026-42945, CVSS v4 score: 9.2) that could allow an attacker to achieve remote code execution or cause a

items.title: ISC Stormcast For Thursday, May 14th, 2026 https://isc.sans.edu/podcastdetail/9932, (Thu, May 14th)
items.description: (c) SANS Internet Storm Center. https://isc.sans.edu Creative Commons Attribution-Noncommercial 3.0 United States License.

items.title: West Pharmaceutical says hackers stole data, encrypted systems
items.description: West Pharmaceutical Services disclosed that it was the target of a cyberattack that resulted in data exfiltration and system encryption. [...]

items.title: Iranian hackers targeted major South Korean electronics maker
items.description: The Iran-linked hacking group MuddyWater (a.k.a. Seedworm, Static Kitten) launched a broad cyber-espionage campaign targeting at least nine high-profile organizations across multiple sectors and countries. [...]

items.title: Checkbox Assessments Aren't Fit to Measure to Risk
items.description: Security governance needs to be more than an annual compliance exercise. New companies are emerging to address risk-management gaps in current audit tools.

items.title: Attackers Weaponize RubyGems for Data Dead Drops
items.description: Threat actors are publishing RubyGems packages that include scrapers targeting public-facing UK government servers, but with no clear objective.

items.title: Tables Turn on 'The Gentlemen' RaaS Gang With Data Leak
items.description: An OPSEC failure provides a window into what helped the ransomware group rise: a generous affiliate model, opportunistic TTPs, and an effective organizational structure.

items.title: New critical Exim mailer flaw allows remote code execution
items.description: A critical vulnerability affecting certain configurations of the Exim open-source mail transfer agent could be exploited by an unauthenticated remote attacker to execute arbitrary code. [...]

items.title: Alleged Dream Market admin arrested in Germany after US indictment
items.description: Court documents said Dream Market was launched in 2013 by Owe Martin Andresen and others before becoming one of the biggest criminal marketplaces online.

items.title: Dark Reading Celebrates 20 Years as a Leading Authority on Cybersecurity, Highlighting the People, Events, Ideas, and Technologies Shaping the Modern Risk Landscape
items.description: Informa TechTarget's flagship cybersecurity media brand launches a special content series to mark two decades as a trusted source for cybersecurity professionals.

items.title: European Commission head pushes creation of new law delaying teens’ social media access
items.description: The comments come as several European countries, including Spain, Greece, Norway, France, Denmark, Turkey and the Netherlands have said they are considering or are implementing age verification protocols to restrict young teens from accessing social media platforms.

items.title: Foxconn Confirms North American Factories Hit by Cyberattack
items.description: The Nitrogen ransomware group claims to have hacked the company’s systems, stealing 8TB of data, including confidential documents. The post Foxconn Confirms North American Factories Hit by Cyberattack appeared first on SecurityWeek.

items.title: Windows BitLocker zero-day gives access to protected drives, PoC released
items.description: A cybersecurity researcher has published proof-of-concept (PoC) exploits for two unpatched Microsoft Windows vulnerabilities named YellowKey and GreenPlasma, which are a BitLocker bypass and a privilege-escalation flaw. [...]

items.title: Microsoft, Palo Alto Networks Find Many Vulnerabilities by Using AI on Their Own Code
items.description: Microsoft’s MDASH discovered 16 of the Patch Tuesday vulnerabilities, and Palo Alto used Mythos to find dozens of flaws. The post Microsoft, Palo Alto Networks Find Many Vulnerabilities by Using AI on Their Own Code appeared first on SecurityWeek.

items.title: Webinar tomorrow: Why security alone won't stop modern attacks
items.description: Tomorrow's webinar examines why prevention alone is no longer enough against modern cyberattacks. The session explores how organizations combine security, backups, and recovery planning to improve cyber resilience after attacks. [...]

items.title: Microsoft fixes BitLocker recovery issue only for Windows 11 users
items.description: Microsoft has addressed a known issue causing some Windows 11 systems to boot into BitLocker recovery after installing the April 2026 Windows security updates. [...]

items.title: Sweet Security Launches Agentic AI Red Teaming to Counter ‘Mythos Moment’
items.description: New “Sweet Attack” platform uses runtime intelligence and continuous agentic red teaming to identify exploitable attack chains human teams may miss. The post Sweet Security Launches Agentic AI Red Teaming to Counter ‘Mythos Moment’ appeared first on SecurityWeek.

items.title: Microsoft fixes Windows Autopatch bug installing restricted drivers
items.description: Microsoft has fixed a Windows Autopatch bug that caused driver updates restricted by administrative policies to be deployed on some Autopatch-managed Windows devices in the European Union. [...]

items.title: Microsoft's MDASH AI System Finds 16 Windows Flaws Fixed in Patch Tuesday
items.description: Microsoft has unveiled a new multi-model artificial intelligence (AI)-driven system called MDASH to facilitate vulnerability discovery and remediation at scale, adding that it's being tested by some customers as part of a limited private preview. MDASH, short for multi-model agentic scanning harness, is designed as a model-agnostic system that uses bespoke AI agents for different vulnerability

items.title: Webinar Today: ROI for Cyber-Physical Security Programs
items.description: This webinar will help OT security teams and asset owners stop being cost centers and start being resilience drivers. The post Webinar Today: ROI for Cyber-Physical Security Programs appeared first on SecurityWeek.

items.title: Azerbaijani Energy Firm Hit by Repeated Microsoft Exchange Exploitation
items.description: A threat actor with affiliations to China has been linked to a "multi-wave intrusion" targeting an unnamed Azerbaijani oil and gas company between late December 2025 and late February 2026, marking an expansion of its targeting. The activity has been attributed by Bitdefender with moderate-to-high confidence to a hacking group known as FamousSparrow (aka UAT-9244), which shares some level of

items.title: China's 'FamousSparrow' APT Nests in South Caucasus Energy Firm
items.description: The cyberthreat group targets an Azerbaijani oil and gas firm with repeated attacks, as the China-linked actors extend targeting beyond hospitality, telecom, and government sectors.

items.title: LatAm Vibe Hackers Generate Custom Hacking Tools on the Fly
items.description: In the latest evolution of automated cyberattacks, two threat campaigns heavily leveraged AI agents to support attacks against entities in Mexico and Brazil.

items.title: UK moves to shield security researchers in cybercrime law overhaul
items.description: The proposed reforms, outlined in briefing documents published alongside the King’s Speech opening a new parliamentary session, would update the Computer Misuse Act 1990 as part of a broader national security package focused on cybercrime and digital threats.

items.title: Microsoft on pace to break annual vulnerability record as AI-driven patch wave takes hold
items.description: Five months into 2026, Microsoft has already patched more than 500 vulnerabilities — although the exact monthly count varies depending on whether analysts include Edge, Chromium and fixes shipped earlier in the month.

items.title: Government to Scrutinize Instructure Over Canvas Disruption, Data Breach
items.description: The Committee on Homeland Security has requested to be briefed on the incident and Instructure’s remediation steps. The post Government to Scrutinize Instructure Over Canvas Disruption, Data Breach appeared first on SecurityWeek.

items.title: [Webinar] How Modern Attack Paths Cross Code, Pipelines, and Cloud
items.description: TL;DR: Stop chasing thousands of "toast" alerts. Join experts from Wiz to learn how hackers connect tiny flaws to build a "Lethal Chain" to your data—and how to break it. Register for the Strategic Briefing Here. Most security tools work like a smoke alarm that goes off every time you burn a piece of toast. You get so many alerts that you eventually start to ignore them. The real danger? While

items.title: Most Remediation Programs Never Confirm the Fix Actually Worked
items.description: Security teams have never had better visibility into their environments and never been worse at confirming what they fix stays fixed. Mandiant's M-Trends 2026 report puts the mean time to exploit at an estimated negative seven days. The Verizon 2025 DBIR puts median time to remediate edge device vulnerabilities at 32 days. These numbers have understandably driven the industry toward a clear

items.title: Microsoft Patches 138 Vulnerabilities, Including DNS and Netlogon RCE Flaws
items.description: Microsoft on Tuesday released patches for 138 security vulnerabilities spanning its product portfolio, although none of them have been listed as publicly known or under active attack. Of the 138 flaws, 30 are rated Critical, 104 are rated Important, three are rated Moderate, and one is rated Low in severity. As many as 61 vulnerabilities are classified as privilege escalation bugs, followed by

items.title: GemStuffer Abuses 150+ RubyGems to Exfiltrate Scraped U.K. Council Portal Data
items.description: Cybersecurity researchers are calling attention to a new campaign dubbed GemStuffer that has targeted the RubyGems repository with more than 150 gems that use the registry as a data exfiltration channel rather than for malware distribution. "The packages do not appear designed for mass developer compromise," Socket said. "Many have little or no download activity, and the payloads are repetitive,

items.title: Android Adds Intrusion Logging for Sophisticated Spyware Forensics
items.description: Google on Tuesday unveiled a new opt-in Android feature called Intrusion Logging for storing forensic logs to better analyze sophisticated spyware attacks. Intrusion Logging, available as part of Advanced Protection Mode, enables "persistent and privacy-preserving forensics logging to allow for investigation of devices in the event of a suspected compromise," the company said. The feature, it

items.title: [GUEST DIARY] Tearing apart website fraud to see how it works., (Wed, May 13th)
items.description: &&#x23;x26;&#x23;x5b;This is a Guest Diary by Joshua Nikolson, an ISC Intern and part of the SANS.edu Bachelor&&#x23;x26;&#x23;39;s degree in Applied Cybersecurity (BACS) program.]

items.description: (c) SANS Internet Storm Center. https://isc.sans.edu Creative Commons Attribution-Noncommercial 3.0 United States License.

items.title: Proxying the Unproxyable? Sending EXE traffic to a Proxy, (Wed, May 13th)
items.description: .. if â€œunproxyableâ€ is a word that is ..

items.title: Patch Tuesday, May 2026 Edition
items.description: Artificial intelligence platforms may be just as susceptible to social engineering as human beings, but they are proving remarkably good at finding security vulnerabilities in human-made computer code. That reality is on full display this month with some of the more widely-used software makers -- including Apple, Google, Microsoft, Mozilla and Oracle -- fixing near record volumes of security bugs, and/or quickening the tempo of their patch releases.

items.title: It's Patch Tuesday for Microsoft & Not a Zero-Day In Sight
items.description: It's the first time in two years with no zero-days. But with 137 flaws to patch, including nine critical ones, admins still have plenty of work to do.

items.title: Foxconn confirms cyberattack impacting North American factories
items.description: A spokesperson for the company confirmed the incident but declined to provide specifics on how many factories in North America were impacted. Foxconn has factories in Wisconsin, Ohio, Texas, Virginia, Indiana and several across Mexico.

items.title: Microsoft May 2026 Patch Tuesday, (Tue, May 12th)
items.description: Today&&#x23;x26;&#x23;39;s Microsoft patch Tuesday fixes 137 different vulnerabilities. In addition, the update addresses 137 Chromium-related issues affecting Microsoft Edge.

items.title: Hugging Face Packages Weaponized With a Single File Tweak
items.description: A tokenizer library file present in Hugging Face AI models can be manipulated to hijack the model's outputs and exfiltrate data.

items.title: 20 Leaders Who Built the CISO Era: 2 Decades of Change
items.description: As part of Dark Reading's 20th anniversary special coverage, we profile the CISOs, founders, researchers, criminals, and policymakers who rewrote the enterprise risk playbook.

items.title: Worm Redux: Fresh Mini Shai-Hulud Infections Bite Supply Chain
items.description: Hundreds of npm packages infected by the self-propagating, credential-stealing worm from TeamPCP are related to the open source TanStack ecosystem.

items.title: ISC Stormcast For Tuesday, May 12th, 2026 https://isc.sans.edu/podcastdetail/9928, (Tue, May 12th)
items.description: (c) SANS Internet Storm Center. https://isc.sans.edu Creative Commons Attribution-Noncommercial 3.0 United States License.

items.title: Apple Patches Everything, (Mon, May 11th)
items.description: Apple today released its typical feature update across it&&#x23;x26;&#x23;39;s operating systems (iOS, iPadOS, macOS, tvOS, watchOS, vision OS). With this update, Apple patched 84 different vulnerabilities. Updates are available for the "26" series of operating systems, as well as for the previous "18" version of iOS/iPadOS, and two versions back for macOS (version 14 and 15).

items.title: Why we use CAPTCHAs, (Mon, May 11th)
items.description: A few months ago, I implemented Cloudflare&&#x23;x26;&#x23;39;s Turnstile CAPTCHA on some pages. The reason for implementing these CAPTCHAs is obvious: Bots make up a large percentage of traffic and affect site performance.

items.title: ISC Stormcast For Monday, May 11th, 2026 https://isc.sans.edu/podcastdetail/9926, (Mon, May 11th)
items.description: (c) SANS Internet Storm Center. https://isc.sans.edu Creative Commons Attribution-Noncommercial 3.0 United States License.

items.title: Canvas Breach Disrupts Schools & Colleges Nationwide
items.description: An ongoing data extortion attack targeting the widely-used education technology platform Canvas disrupted classes and coursework at school districts and universities across the United States today, after a cybercrime group defaced the service's login page with a ransom demand that threatened to leak data from 275 million students and faculty across nearly 9,000 educational institutions.

items.title: CISA Unveils New Initiative to Fortify America’s Critical Infrastructure
CISA, US and International Partners Release Guide to Secure Adoption of Agentic AI
Anti-DDoS Firm Heaped Attacks on Brazilian ISPs
items.description: A Brazilian tech firm that specializes in protecting networks from distributed denial-of-service (DDoS) attacks has been enabling a botnet responsible for an extended campaign of massive DDoS attacks against other network operators in Brazil, KrebsOnSecurity has learned. The firm's chief executive says the malicious activity resulted from a security breach and was likely the work of a competitor trying to tarnish his company's public image.

items.title: CISA and U.S. Government Partners Unveil Guide to Accelerate Zero Trust Adoption in Operational Technology
CISA, National Cyber Security Centre (NCSC) UK, and Global Partners Issue Advisory on Chinese Government-Linked Covert Cyber Networks
CISA Warns of FIRESTARTER Malware Targeting Cisco ASA including Firepower and Secure Firewall Products
‘Scattered Spider’ Member ‘Tylerb’ Pleads Guilty
items.description: A 24-year-old British national and senior member of the cybercrime group "Scattered Spider" has pleaded guilty to wire fraud conspiracy and aggravated identity theft. Tyler Robert Buchanan admitted his role in a series of text-message phishing attacks in the summer of 2022 that allowed the group to hack into at least a dozen major technology companies and steal tens of millions of dollars worth of cryptocurrency from investors.

items.title: Patch Tuesday, April 2026 Edition
items.description: Microsoft today pushed software updates to fix a staggering 167 security vulnerabilities in its Windows operating systems and related software, including a SharePoint Server zero-day and a publicly disclosed weakness in Windows Defender dubbed "BlueHammer." Separately, Google Chrome fixed its fourth zero-day of 2026, and an emergency update for Adobe Reader nixes an actively exploited flaw that can lead to remote code execution.

items.title: Russia Hacked Routers to Steal Microsoft Office Tokens
items.description: Hackers linked to Russia's military intelligence units are using known flaws in older Internet routers to mass harvest authentication tokens from Microsoft Office users, security experts warned today. The spying campaign allowed state-backed Russian hackers to quietly siphon authentication tokens from users on more than 18,000 networks without deploying any malicious software or code.

items.title: Germany Doxes “UNKN,” Head of RU Ransomware Gangs REvil, GandCrab
items.description: An elusive hacker who went by the handle "UNKN" and ran the early Russian ransomware groups GandCrab and REvil now has a name and a face. Authorities in Germany say 31-year-old Russian Daniil Maksimovich Shchukin headed both cybercrime gangs and helped carry out at least 130 acts of computer sabotage and extortion against victims across the country between 2019 and 2021.

items.title: ‘CanisterWorm’ Springs Wiper Attack Targeting Iran
items.description: A financially motivated data theft and extortion group is attempting to inject itself into the Iran war, unleashing a worm that spreads through poorly secured cloud services and wipes data on infected systems that use Iran's time zone or have Farsi set as the default language.

items.title: Feds Disrupt IoT Botnets Behind Huge DDoS Attacks
items.description: The U.S. Justice Department joined authorities in Canada and Germany in dismantling the online infrastructure behind four highly disruptive botnets that compromised more than three million hacked Internet of Things (IoT) devices, such as routers and web cameras. The feds say the four botnets -- named Aisuru, Kimwolf, JackSkid and Mossad -- are responsible for a series of recent record-smashing distributed denial-of-service (DDoS) attacks capable of knocking nearly any target offline.

items.title: Iran-Backed Hackers Claim Wiper Attack on Medtech Firm Stryker
items.description: A hacktivist group with links to Iran's intelligence agencies is claiming responsibility for a data-wiping attack against Stryker, a global medical technology company based in Michigan. News reports out of Ireland, Stryker's largest hub outside of the United States, said the company sent home more than 5,000 workers there today. Meanwhile, a voicemail message at Stryker's main U.S. headquarters says the company is currently experiencing a building emergency.

items.title: CISA Issues Updated RESURGE Malware Analysis Highlighting a Stealthy but Active Threat
Immediate Action Required: CISA Issues Emergency Directive to Secure Cisco SD-WAN Systems
CISA Announces New Town Halls to Engage with Stakeholders on Cyber Incident Reporting for Critical Infrastructure
CISA’s 2025 Year in Review: Driving Security and Resilience Across Critical Infrastructure
CISA Releases Guide to Help Critical Infrastructure Users Adopt More Secure Communication
Student Loan Breach Exposes 2.5M Records
items.description: 2.5 million people were affected, in a breach that could spell more trouble down the line.

items.title: Watering Hole Attacks Push ScanBox Keylogger
items.description: Researchers uncover a watering hole attack likely carried out by APT TA423, which attempts to plant the ScanBox JavaScript-based reconnaissance tool.

items.title: Tentacles of ‘0ktapus’ Threat Group Victimize 130 Firms
items.description: Over 130 companies tangled in sprawling phishing campaign that spoofed a multi-factor authentication system.

items.title: Ransomware Attacks are on the Rise
items.description: Lockbit is by far this summer’s most prolific ransomware group, trailed by two offshoots of the Conti group.

items.title: Cybercriminals Are Selling Access to Chinese Surveillance Cameras
items.description: Tens of thousands of cameras have failed to patch a critical, 11-month-old CVE, leaving thousands of organizations exposed.

items.title: Twitter Whistleblower Complaint: The TL;DR Version
items.description: Twitter is blasted for security and privacy lapses by the company’s former head of security who alleges the social media giant’s actions amount to a national security risk.

items.title: Firewall Bug Under Active Attack Triggers CISA Warning
items.description: CISA is warning that Palo Alto Networks’ PAN-OS is under active attack and needs to be patched ASAP.

items.title: Fake Reservation Links Prey on Weary Travelers
items.description: Fake travel reservations are exacting more pain from the travel weary, already dealing with the misery of canceled flights and overbooked hotels.

items.title: iPhone Users Urged to Update to Patch 2 Zero-Days
items.description: Separate fixes to macOS and iOS patch respective flaws in the kernel and WebKit that can allow threat actors to take over devices and are under attack.

items.title: Google Patches Chrome’s Fifth Zero-Day of the Year
items.description: An insufficient validation input flaw, one of 11 patched in an update this week, could allow for arbitrary code execution and is under active attack.
